title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe their experience of reducing their use of digital devices. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Switching Off: Four People on Digital Detox and Screen Time

texts.A:
### Fiona, 37, Marketing Director
My job is essentially impossible without constant connectivity – I manage campaigns across multiple platforms and my phone never really stops. [43]I attended a week-long digital detox retreat in the Scottish Highlands last summer where phones were collected on arrival and returned only on departure.[/43] The first two days were genuinely uncomfortable – I kept reaching for a pocket that was empty. [47]What I struggled with most was the irrational fear that I was falling behind professionally, even though I knew perfectly well that the world would continue without my input for seven days.[/47] By day three something shifted, and I read two novels, walked for hours and had real conversations. [51]Since returning, I've introduced a strict no-phone rule after eight in the evening, which has improved both my sleep and my relationship with my partner in ways I didn't fully predict.[/51]
texts.B:
### Joel, 22, University Student
[44]I realised I had a problem with screen time when I checked my usage statistics and found I was spending over eight hours a day on my phone, almost entirely scrolling social media.[/44] At university I was supposed to be studying, but I was mostly consuming content I couldn't have recalled an hour later. I deleted the three most compulsive apps and replaced them with a reading habit. [48]After two weeks I noticed I was sleeping more deeply and feeling notably less anxious – I hadn't expected such a physical response to what I thought was a small behavioural change.[/48] [52]The hardest part is that social media is also how I stay connected with friends from home, so opting out has come with a genuine social cost that I hadn't fully considered.[/52]
texts.C:
### Beatrice, 45, General Practitioner
[45]In my surgery I'm increasingly seeing patients – particularly teenagers and young adults – presenting with sleep problems, anxiety and poor concentration that have a clear link to excessive screen use.[/45] I now ask patients about their device habits in the same way I ask about diet and exercise, because the evidence is increasingly hard to ignore. [49]I tried a partial digital detox last year – I kept my work phone but deleted social media entirely – and within a few weeks I noticed I was finishing books again and feeling less mentally cluttered.[/49] Having experienced the benefits personally, I'm far more confident recommending it to patients.
texts.D:
### Tariq, 58, Secondary School Principal
[46]We introduced a full phone-free school day two years ago, which meant students surrendering their phones at the gate each morning and collecting them at the end of the day.[/46] The decision was controversial with parents at first – several worried about not being able to reach their children in an emergency. [50]Within one term, teachers reported a significant improvement in concentration in lessons and a noticeable reduction in the low-level social conflicts that phone use had been fuelling between students.[/50] I've also observed that students are simply talking to each other at lunchtime again, and several neighbouring schools have since asked us to share our approach.

questions.question: attended an organised programme specifically designed to separate them from their devices?
questions.explanation: Fiona attended a week-long digital detox retreat where phones were collected on arrival and returned only on departure.

questions.question: only realised the extent of their screen use after looking at concrete data about their habits?
questions.explanation: Joel says he realised he had a problem when he checked his usage statistics and saw he was spending over eight hours a day on his phone.

questions.question: describes observing a clear connection between device use and health problems in the people they work with professionally?
questions.explanation: Beatrice says she increasingly sees patients with sleep problems, anxiety and poor concentration that have a clear link to excessive screen use.

questions.question: introduced a rule about device use in an institution they are responsible for?
questions.explanation: Tariq introduced a full phone-free school day, with students surrendering their phones at the gate each morning.

questions.question: describes experiencing an irrational concern about professional consequences while offline?
questions.explanation: Fiona says she struggled with the irrational fear of falling behind professionally, even knowing the world would continue without her for seven days.

questions.question: was surprised by a physical improvement that resulted from changing their digital habits?
questions.explanation: Joel hadn't expected such a physical response – deeper sleep and notably less anxiety – from what he thought was a small behavioural change.

questions.question: says that personal experience of reducing screen time has made them more effective at advising others?
questions.explanation: Beatrice says that having experienced the benefits personally, she is far more confident recommending a digital detox to patients.

questions.question: describes measurable improvements in behaviour and relationships within an institution after removing devices?
questions.explanation: Tariq says teachers reported improved concentration in lessons and a reduction in social conflicts within one term of introducing the phone-free day.

questions.question: says a change in behaviour at a particular time of day has had a positive effect on their personal relationships?
questions.explanation: Fiona introduced a no-phone rule after eight in the evening, which improved her sleep and her relationship with her partner.

questions.question: acknowledges a genuine social downside to reducing their use of certain digital platforms?
questions.explanation: Joel says social media is how he stays connected with friends from home, so opting out has come with a genuine social cost.
